author: Dr. Olivia Reyes (Emergency Medicine)
content:
EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT NOTE — UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
Date/Time: 3/10/2027, 1:30 PM
Patient: Whitlock, Sarah L (MRN-90216055), 50F

CHIEF COMPLAINT: Worsening abdominal distension and difficulty breathing.

HPI: 50-year-old woman with decompensated alcohol-related cirrhosis (hepatic encephalopathy, ascites, right hepatic hydrothorax; ~14 months sober, in transplant evaluation) presents with progressive abdominal distension and dyspnea over the past week. Her hepatologist (Dr. Hale) has been attempting to increase her furosemide and spironolactone to control the hydrothorax, but higher doses produced worsening kidney function, hyperkalemia, and hyponatremia (see 2/12 hepatology labs/note). Because she cannot tolerate diuretic escalation and is now symptomatic, Dr. Hale referred her to the ED for therapeutic thoracentesis and paracentesis. She denies fever, chest pain, hematemesis, melena, or confusion today. She lives ~90 minutes away and was driven in by her sister.

HOME MEDICATIONS: spironolactone 50 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, lactulose three times daily, rifaximin 550 mg three times daily, thiamine, folate.

EXAM:
- Vitals: T 98.0, HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air. Vital signs stable aside from the mildly elevated respiratory rate.
- General: mild respiratory effort, speaking in full sentences, no acute distress at rest.
- Abdomen: distended with a positive fluid wave; non-tender; no rebound.
- Chest: absent breath sounds at the right base with dullness to percussion; left clear.
- Neuro: alert, oriented x3; no asterixis today.

DATA:
- Labs (LAB001): Na 128, K 4.6, BUN 32, Cr 1.4, bilirubin 4.5, albumin 2.6, INR 2.0, platelets 55 K, Hgb 10.1, ammonia 62. MELD-Na ~27.
- Abdominal US (IMG001): cirrhotic liver, splenomegaly, large-volume ascites, patent portal vein.
- CXR (IMG002): moderate-to-large right pleural effusion consistent with hepatic hydrothorax.

ASSESSMENT/PLAN:
50F with decompensated cirrhosis, symptomatic from diuretic-refractory ascites and right hepatic hydrothorax, referred for mechanical drainage after diuretic intolerance.
- Interventional radiology consulted for therapeutic large-volume paracentesis and right thoracentesis.
- Send ascitic fluid studies (cell count/differential, albumin, culture) to evaluate for SBP.
- Continue home lactulose/rifaximin; hold further diuretic escalation.
- Disposition: anticipate same-day discharge home after drainage if she returns to baseline.
attestation: Electronically signed by Dr. Olivia Reyes, MD (Emergency Medicine). 3/10/2027.
